reasoning_summary: For live run 20260421_182124_bf16_gemm_v1_49dfa799 at 26.306592 ms, round 2 restored the compact PTX hot-band body but not the accepted performance surface. Relative to the accepted PTX base 20260421_172601_bf16_gemm_v1_117cd3e7 at 24.806945 ms, registers and shared memory are unchanged at 168 regs/thread and 22016 B/block, while barrier rises from 6.39% to 9.73%, short-scoreboard rises from 1.86% to 7.32%, tensor activity drops from 48.36% to 46.63%, and the deliberate code delta on the hot path is the new __launch_bounds__(128, 3). That makes a launch-bounds rollback the highest-confidence next move. I rank a final-drain wait/sync split second because the PTX loop still pays a wait_group_0 plus __syncthreads handoff even when no future refill is issued, and I keep a grouped_rows=2 launch-order probe third as the next bounded alternative after the launch-bounds regression is isolated. I defer any 256x128 reopen or shared-memory stage growth until the compact PTX anchor is back near its accepted baseline.
family_audit: accept: keep the compact PTX 128x128 hot-band family active because the latest regression versus 117cd3e7 is concentrated in launch-bounds and synchronization, not in the row-pair PTX kernel body itself.
defer: do not reopen the 256x128 or wider tiling families this round; the current loss is local to the PTX launch/sync surface and should be deconfounded before another large geometry branch.

reject_this_round: avoid any stage-depth or export-scratch growth because the current 22016 B shared-memory footprint is already the safer surface and prior larger shared-memory probes did not beat the accepted PTX base.

directions.name: Restore PTX Launch Bounds Back To 2-CTA On The Active Hot-Band Path
directions.hypothesis: The current run already restored the compact PTX hot-band dispatch and preserved the 22016 B shared-memory footprint, but it still trails the accepted PTX base by about 1.50 ms while carrying the exact same 168 regs/thread. The main new lever is __launch_bounds__(128, 3), so rolling that back to 2 should recover scheduler freedom and remove the extra barrier plus short-scoreboard tax without touching grouped rows, stage depth, or the row-pair accumulator path.
directions.expected_bottleneck: The explicit 3-CTA minimum is over-constraining the PTX microkernel schedule, inflating barrier and short-scoreboard even though it does not reduce the measured register footprint.
directions.risk: Low. This is a one-line rollback on the live PTX surface and keeps the current dispatch, shared-memory footprint, and row-pair math path intact.
ranking_notes: This is the cleanest single-variable experiment because the accepted PTX base and the current run share the same 168 regs/thread and 22016 B/block footprint.
The accepted-base comparison points straight at launch policy: barrier is +3.34 pts, short-scoreboard is +5.46 pts, and tensor activity is lower even though occupancy_limit_registers improved to 3.
It preserves the row-pair PTX kernel body that already beat CUTLASS and only removes the new residency forcing lever.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if reverting launch_bounds to 2 does not materially recover barrier or short-scoreboard versus 49dfa799, or if more than the launch-bounds annotation has to change to build.
expected_local_changes: Retune the PTX hot-band __launch_bounds__ annotation from 3 back to 2.
Leave the active PTX dispatch in place.
Leave grouped rows, shared-memory stage depth, and export scratch untouched in this validation pass.

directions.name: Split The Final PTX Wait/Sync Drain Out Of The Steady-State Loop
directions.hypothesis: The PTX steady-state loop always executes cp_async_wait_group_0 plus __syncthreads whenever next_tile_idx < FixedKTiles, even on the penultimate tile where future_tile_idx is already out of range and no refill is issued. Pulling that no-refill drain into a small epilogue case should cut one barrier seam without adding another shared-memory stage or touching the active PTX dispatch.
directions.expected_bottleneck: The final steady-state handoff is paying an unnecessary wait/sync pair that shows up as barrier and short-scoreboard tax on the PTX path.
directions.risk: Moderate. The change is still local, but it touches the PTX hot loop control flow and can introduce off-by-one or pipeline-drain correctness bugs if the epilogue split is wrong.
ranking_notes: Barrier is still 9.73% even after restoring the PTX surface, so the hottest remaining local lever is the steady-state wait/sync seam itself.
This idea preserves the 22016 B shared-memory budget and avoids reopening the failed two-K-stage x32 buffer family.
I rank it below the launch-bounds rollback because the accepted-base comparison says the current loss is still dominated by the newly introduced launch policy.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if splitting the drain requires another shared-memory stage, a wider launch rewrite, or extra hot-loop predicates beyond a tiny epilogue case.
expected_local_changes: Split the no-refill drain from the current steady-state wait/sync block.
Keep the active PTX dispatch and 22016 B shared-memory budget unchanged.
Leave grouped rows and the row-pair accumulator schedule untouched.

directions.name: Retune PTX Hot-Band Grouped Rows From 4 Down To 2
directions.hypothesis: With the PTX dispatch back on the hot band, the current run still shows high barrier plus short-scoreboard while DRAM and L2 throughput both climbed. That pattern is consistent with the grouped_rows=4 launch order batching too much work per CTA group. Cutting the PTX grouped-row depth to 2 is the bounded opposite-direction probe that trades a little B reuse for a shorter barrier seam and faster handoff cadence.
directions.expected_bottleneck: PTX grouped-row batching may be over-amortizing locality and paying too much synchronization and scheduler delay per group.
directions.risk: Moderate-high. This changes the launch-order geometry for the active PTX kernel and can easily lose locality if the grouped_rows reduction is too aggressive.
ranking_notes: This is the cheapest launch-order counterprobe after the launch-bounds rollback because it moves only one constant and the corresponding PTX grouping math.
The current metrics show more memory traffic but worse synchronization than the accepted PTX base, which is the regime where smaller CTA groups are worth testing.
I rank it third because the older grouped_rows family already produced mixed evidence, so it should follow after the simpler launch-bounds isolation experiment.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if grouped_rows=2 reduces locality enough to spike DRAM or L2 pressure without a clear barrier improvement, or if it forces another dispatch-family rewrite.
expected_local_changes: Keep the active PTX dispatch and row-pair math schedule unchanged.
Use the existing PTX grouping math with the new constant rather than reopening the regular 128x128 sibling.

notes: Round 3 treats 49dfa799 as a local launch-policy regression on top of the compact PTX base, not as a reason to abandon the PTX hot-band family. The next move should isolate launch_bounds before reopening larger geometry or shared-memory families.
